title: Density and pressure
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master density, hydrostatic pressure and Archimedes' principle for Cambridge 9702 AS Physics with worked examples and exam tips.
learningObjectives: Define density and pressure and apply the equations ρ = m/V and p = F/A correctly in calculation and conceptual questions
Derive and use the hydrostatic pressure equation p = ρgh to solve problems involving pressure at depth in a fluid
Explain upthrust using Archimedes' principle and apply it to floating, sinking and partially submerged objects

simpleExplanation.title: Density & Pressure in 60 seconds
simpleExplanation.summary: Density tells you how much mass is packed into a given volume — a brick and a sponge may be the same size, but the brick has far more mass crammed in. Pressure is the force pushing on each square metre of a surface, and in a fluid it increases the deeper you go because more fluid is stacked above. Master three equations and you can crack almost every question on this topic.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine standing on a thick mattress versus stiletto heels — the same body weight spread over a large boot sole barely dents the mattress, but a tiny heel sinks straight in because all that force is concentrated on a tiny area. That is pressure in action.
steps: Learn the three core equations: ρ = m/V, p = F/A, and p = ρgh — know every symbol and its SI unit
For pressure-at-depth problems, identify the fluid density, gravitational field strength (9.81 m s⁻²), and the vertical depth — never use slant distance
For upthrust, remember it equals the weight of fluid displaced: U = ρ_fluid × V_submerged × g
Check your answer makes physical sense — deeper always means higher pressure; a less dense object floats on a denser fluid

sections.content: Pressure and density sit at the heart of fluid mechanics and appear consistently across Paper 1 MCQs and Paper 2 structured questions in Cambridge 9702. Whether you are calculating the pressure on a submarine hull, explaining why an iceberg floats, or comparing materials by their **density**, you need a tight grip on three concise equations. This lesson builds each concept from first principles, walks through the examiner's favourite question styles, and flags the small mistakes that cost students marks every year.

**Density** (ρ, pronounced 'rho') is defined as the mass per unit volume of a substance. It is a scalar quantity and its SI unit is kg m⁻³. A high density means a large amount of mass occupies a small volume — this is why lead feels so much heavier than polystyrene for the same sized block. Density is an intrinsic property of a material; it does not change if you cut the material in half (both halves have the same density, though each has half the mass and half the volume). Common reference values: water ≈ 1000 kg m⁻³, air ≈ 1.2 kg m⁻³, aluminium ≈ 2700 kg m⁻³.
ρ = m / V   [units: kg m⁻³, where m is mass in kg and V is volume in m³]

**Pressure** (p) is defined as the normal force acting per unit area of a surface. It is a scalar quantity and its SI unit is the pascal (Pa), where 1 Pa = 1 N m⁻². The force must act perpendicularly (normally) to the surface — any tangential component does not contribute to pressure. Pressure appears in many contexts: gas pressure inside a cylinder, blood pressure in arteries, and hydrostatic pressure at depth in a lake. When a question mentions 'stress on a surface' and gives a force and area, reach straight for p = F/A.
p = F / A   [units: Pa = N m⁻², where F is the normal force in N and A is the area in m²]

In a static fluid, pressure increases with depth because every extra layer of fluid above a point adds more weight per unit area. Consider a horizontal surface at depth h below the free surface of a fluid of density ρ. The weight of a fluid column of cross-sectional area A and height h is W = ρAhg. Dividing by area A gives the pressure due to the fluid alone: p = ρgh. This is the **gauge pressure** — the pressure above atmospheric. The **absolute pressure** at depth h is p_abs = p_atm + ρgh. Key insight: pressure at a given depth depends only on the fluid density and the vertical depth, not on the shape or width of the container (Pascal's vases).
p = ρgh   [gauge pressure in Pa; ρ in kg m⁻³, g = 9.81 m s⁻², h = vertical depth in m]

When an object is partially or fully submerged in a fluid, the fluid exerts a net upward force called **upthrust** (or buoyancy force). This arises because pressure increases with depth: the pressure on the bottom face of the object is greater than on the top face, producing a net upward force. **Archimedes' principle** states that the upthrust on a body equals the weight of fluid displaced by that body. For a fully submerged object of volume V in a fluid of density ρ_f: U = ρ_f V g. An object floats when upthrust equals its weight; it sinks when its weight exceeds the maximum possible upthrust (i.e. when its average density exceeds the fluid density).
U = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g   [upthrust in N; use the submerged volume, not the total object volume if partially submerged]

items: Always convert cm³ → m³ (divide by 10⁶) and g cm⁻³ → kg m⁻³ (multiply by 1000) before substituting into equations — unit errors are the most common source of lost marks.
Hydrostatic pressure depends on vertical depth h, not path length — if a tube is inclined, use the vertical height difference, not the length along the tube.
Gauge pressure and absolute pressure are different: gauge pressure = ρgh; absolute pressure = atmospheric pressure + ρgh. Read the question carefully to see which is required.
Upthrust uses the density of the FLUID and the volume of fluid displaced — not the density or volume of the object itself (unless it is fully submerged).
For a floating object: weight of object = upthrust, so m_object × g = ρ_fluid × V_submerged × g. The g cancels, giving a clean ratio: V_submerged / V_total = ρ_object / ρ_fluid.
The pascal is a derived unit: 1 Pa = 1 N m⁻² = 1 kg m⁻¹ s⁻². You may be asked to show this in a units question on Paper 2.

sections.question: A rectangular block of wood has dimensions 0.20 m × 0.15 m × 0.080 m and a mass of 1.8 kg. The block is placed in fresh water (density 1000 kg m⁻³). (a) Calculate the density of the wood. (b) Show that the block floats. (c) Calculate the fraction of the block's volume that lies below the water surface.
sections.solution:
Part (a) — Density of the wood:
Volume of block: V = 0.20 × 0.15 × 0.080 = 2.4 × 10⁻³ m³
Density: ρ = m/V = 1.8 / (2.4 × 10⁻³) = 750 kg m⁻³

Part (b) — Does it float?
For the block to float, its density must be less than the fluid density.
ρ_wood = 750 kg m⁻³ < ρ_water = 1000 kg m⁻³ ✓
Therefore the block floats (the maximum upthrust when fully submerged exceeds the block's weight).
Max upthrust = ρ_water × V × g = 1000 × 2.4 × 10⁻³ × 9.81 = 23.5 N
Weight of block = mg = 1.8 × 9.81 = 17.7 N
Since 23.5 N > 17.7 N, the block floats. ✓

Part (c) — Fraction submerged:
At equilibrium: upthrust = weight
ρ_water × V_sub × g = m × g  →  g cancels
V_sub = m / ρ_water = 1.8 / 1000 = 1.8 × 10⁻³ m³
Fraction = V_sub / V_total = (1.8 × 10⁻³) / (2.4 × 10⁻³) = 0.75
Alternatively: fraction = ρ_wood / ρ_water = 750 / 1000 = 0.75
75% of the block lies below the water surface.

sections.content: In Paper 1 MCQs on pressure at depth, Cambridge often includes a distractor answer that uses the slant length of an angled pipe rather than the vertical depth. Always ask yourself: 'have I used the vertical height?' If the diagram shows an inclined tube, extract the vertical component before applying p = ρgh. In Paper 2, when 'show that' questions ask you to verify a floating condition, state both the weight AND the maximum upthrust and compare them — a bare equation with no comparison will not score the 'conclusion' mark.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What equations do I need for density and pressure in Cambridge 9702 AS Physics?
faq.a: You need three core equations: ρ = m/V for density, p = F/A for pressure on a surface, and p = ρgh for hydrostatic pressure at depth in a fluid. You should also be able to write upthrust as U = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g using Archimedes' principle. Our free course covers all three with worked examples and past-paper practice so you can apply them confidently under exam conditions.

faq.q: How is gauge pressure different from absolute pressure in 9702 questions?
faq.a: Gauge pressure (p = ρgh) is the pressure due to the fluid column alone — it is the pressure above atmospheric. Absolute pressure is the total pressure including the atmosphere acting on the fluid surface: p_abs = p_atm + ρgh. Cambridge questions usually specify which is required; if they say 'pressure due to the water' or 'additional pressure', they want gauge pressure. If they say 'total pressure' or give atmospheric pressure as data, use absolute pressure. Practise identifying this distinction with our past-paper marking tool.

faq.q: Why does an object float, and how does Cambridge 9702 test Archimedes' principle?
faq.a: An object floats when the upthrust (weight of fluid displaced) equals the object's weight. This happens when the object's average density is less than the fluid's density. Cambridge tests this in two main ways: calculation questions where you find the submerged fraction using ρ_object/ρ_fluid, and 'show that' questions where you must compare the maximum upthrust with the object's weight and draw a conclusion. Our premium lesson walks through both question types with full mark-scheme style solutions.
